sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 541519 as other computer-related services, explicitly includes disaster recovery and software installation, and excludes programming, systems-design, and facilities-management services. It supports the boundary but does not measure eligibility or recurring-revenue prevalence.

The 2022 Economic Census form distinguishes 541519 computer-related consulting and installation from neighboring activities and enumerates technical support, recovery, security, domain, and consulting product lines, supporting the claimed heterogeneity and classification boundary.

The BLS NAICS 541500 table reports computer and mathematical occupations at 50.57% of employment and management occupations at 14.77%, exactly supporting the broader-industry inputs described in the bridge.

BLS describes diagnosis, documentation, equipment setup or repair, hardware and software installation, user training, automation pressure from chatbots, complex-case work, and outsourcing by smaller organizations.

Anthropic reports high automation-mode use in Claude Code and frequent human feedback or validation, supporting the directional software-work exposure claim while remaining limited to observed Claude interactions rather than 541519 production work.
The Management Science abstract reports three randomized field experiments involving 4,867 developers and a combined 26.08% increase in completed tasks with an AI coding assistant.

The report supports AI expansion plans, service customization, mixed delivery tooling, and integration, quality, staffing, and vendor-dependency challenges. Its 290-person worldwide member sample is broader than pure MSPs (only 45% identify their business type as MSP) and is 70% EMEA-focused, weakening representativeness for U.S. 541519.

Kaseya states that its 2026 report surveyed more than 1,000 MSPs worldwide, that 53% use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, that more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload, and that cybersecurity and disaster-recovery revenue grew for many respondents.

The Kaseya pricing page reports the cited predominant billing mix: 26% combined per-user/per-device, 21% per-user, 14% fixed-fee value subscription, 13% per-device, 12% a la carte, 10% tiered bundles, and 3% incident response.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years, and clearly identifies the measure as cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying sales.
The advisory PDF reports 466 global MSP deals in 2025, up about 20%, with $4.3 billion in disclosed value, supporting active MSP consolidation but not a U.S. 541519 transfer rate.
The BLS projection table reports a 3.2% annual real-output growth rate for broader NAICS 541500, supporting the stated five-year compounding proxy and its disclosed population mismatch.

caveats: The labor-opportunity bridge combines broader NAICS 541500 employment shares with software-developer evidence and then converts them into a wage-weighted 541519 task share without exact-industry payroll or workflow data; the PROXY label and wide H range disclose, but do not resolve, that uncertainty.

The rho base projects 65% five-year realization from current adoption and implementation signals in global, vendor- or industry-channel samples. Those sources do not directly measure durable technician-hour elimination, exception rates, or U.S. lower-middle-market deployment.
S7's survey population is materially broader than pure MSPs and is heavily EMEA-weighted, so it is only partial support for a U.S. 541519 implementation bridge.

The q bridge observes predominant billing models, not revenue weights, renewal cadence, competitive pass-through, or retained automation economics. Because every registered q scenario exceeds the factor cap, C remains 10 throughout the envelope despite this unmeasured retention uncertainty.
The s5 bridge combines cross-industry five-year owner intentions with global MSP deal counts; neither source measures completed qualifying control transfers among eligible U.S. 541519 firms.

The demand factor combines a broader 541500 output forecast, vendor-reported MSP service-line growth, and an explicit operator-required-share estimate. Programming and systems-design activities excluded from the lens may account for a different share of broader-category growth than residual 541519 services.

The eligibility and operator-required shares are honest ESTIMATE inputs, but the residual code's broad classification and unknown mix of recurring managed services, project installation, consulting, and pass-through revenue make the base thesis strongly model-conditioned.
summary: Mechanics pass and all 12 primitive-cited sources were reopened. The sources support the cited facts and the record labels its key extrapolations as PROXY or ESTIMATE; no unsupported claim, evidence-state downgrade, lens issue, construct error, or range concern was shown to change the base tier or action under the brief's materiality test. Publication is appropriate with caveats because exact NAICS 541519 evidence is sparse, S7 only partially matches the stated MSP population, and the H, F, C, and D inputs depend heavily on broader-industry, global-MSP, and judgmental bridges.
